pages: Move the pointer over a boundary. A glow appears when that part of the seal can be acted on.

Click a highlighted boundary to select it. Clicking a selected boundary keeps it selected so it can be dragged; hold Shift while clicking or brushing selected parts to deselect them, or click the empty field to clear the selection.
Select the outer boundary of the paired veils. Right-click and choose ‚ÄòEliminate the double cut,‚Äô or press Delete or Backspace, to lift both veils.
There are two paired veils here. Either pair may be lifted first.
A dark field does not preserve every fragment drawn within it. Select one complete inner veil and erase it; the remaining pair is already familiar.
The timeline records every move. Drag its handle backward to revisit an earlier state; making a move there creates a new branch without deleting the earlier attempt.

The innermost veil is an exact echo of the older veil beside its host. Where the older form remains, the supported echo may be removed by deiteration; the familiar clearing and paired release remain afterward.
A ring is not a veil: it names ownership and does not reverse the field. This ring owns no mark, so it may be dissolved.
Dissolve the empty ring. The familiar paired veils remain.
Move the pointer over a mark or boundary. A glow appears when that part of the seal can be acted on.

Click a highlighted part to select it. Clicking a selected part keeps it selected so it can be dragged; hold Shift while clicking or brushing selected parts to deselect them, or click the empty field to clear the selection.
The timeline at the bottom records every move. Drag its handle backward to revisit an earlier state; making a move there creates a new branch without deleting the earlier attempt.

The inner repeated mark has an equal ancestor outside its field, so it may be removed by deiteration. Select that inner mark and choose Deiterate; then clear the remaining negative-field mark and release the empty boundaries.
Good. The Seyric rings are ownership marks, not ornament. That distinction will matter among the Myratic finds.
The rings are nested, but they do not share a mark. Follow each color back to its own ring before lifting either echo.
The marked negative-P fragment already has ancestor support. Remove its exact descendant echo to expose Q, then carry Q through the separate continuation to R.
Treat the three alternatives as flat peers and compare their P/Q multiplicities through the shared rings.
Copy one supported P into the incomplete branch; all three then become the same P-and-P-and-Q alternative, exposing idempotence at both connective levels.
The direct closed theorem (A‚àßB)‚à®(A‚àßC) ‚Üí A‚àß(B‚à®C) presents the shared A inside both source alternatives and asks for one factored A with the residual B/C choice preserved.
(A‚à®B)‚àß(A‚à®C) exposes atomic A as the exact common alternative to factor into A‚à®(B‚àßC).
The whole common alternative K=(P‚à®Q) occurs in both clauses ((K‚à®R)‚àß(K‚à®S)) and must remain intact in K‚à®(R‚àßS).
The direct closed theorem A‚à®(B‚àßC) ‚Üí (A‚à®B)‚àß(A‚à®C) asks the same A alternative to survive in both conjunctive conclusions while B and C remain distinct.
Carry the common A into both alternatives: A‚àß(B‚à®C) expands to (A‚àßB)‚à®(A‚àßC). Keep B and C in separate branches while preserving the same A owner in each.
Compare the flat P-and-Q pair with the equal pair already inside a ring. The owned marks match, but their complete open patterns do not yet have the same boundary.

One useful route wraps exactly the flat pair in a fresh empty ring. That single boundary becomes an ancestor-shaped source for the pre-existing ringed pair; wrapping one mark, wrapping more of the tablet, or using paired veils does not create that ownership match.
The proposition ring names P but does not reverse field polarity. Count only veils as you trace the four P occurrences through alternating hosts.
Keep the outer P as ancestor support while lifting the three inward echoes from deepest to shallowest; then clear that support from its negative field and release the two remaining veil pairs.
Both branches contain the complete P‚à®Q conjunct. Factor it once while preserving the different residuals R and S‚àßT, then use the factored result in the U continuation.
The two marks inside the veil belong to the same ring: this is one conjunct repeated, not two different conditions.
That bright field will not permit ordinary clearing. Lift one exact echo through the matching older mark; the remaining copy can then meet the same support.
The four marked parts form one complete proposition: if P and Q, then R or S. Its nested veils show that grouping; they are not four separate chores.

The tiny empty veil beside it already settles the dark field. Select the outer boundary of the entire marked proposition and erase that whole fragment in one move; then release the empty rings and the remaining paired veil.
This is left injection: the given P supplies the left alternative of P ‚à® Q. The right alternative does not also need a premise.
The missing source content is not two loose marks: it is the complete cut-delimited not-(P-and-Q) fragment inside the T source.
Insert that whole fragment with its boundary and both internal marks intact. Only the completed T source matches the blocked target in the surrounding R context.
Trace the two available source owners into the three disjunction branches.
Both left P and right R are legitimate injections; compare them and reject unsupported Q rather than following a fixed side habit.
The two sibling branches return to the same ring. They repeat one alternative; they do not offer two different choices.
Lift one exact branch as a supported echo. Only then will the remaining branch and its surrounding veil form a pair that can be released.
The sibling branches trade visual positions, but their common field does not give either branch a semantic first place.
Each branch still returns to the same ring as before. This is the same disjunction under a different carving order; no exchange operation is required.
The source already shows P‚à®(Q‚àßR). In the deeper target, wrap exactly the exchanged P and Q‚àßR branches‚Äîleave X outside‚Äîso the missing disjunction boundary matches the source.
A written reader might say ‚ÄúP and Q, then R‚Äù or ‚ÄúP, then Q and R.‚Äù The tablet cuts no boundary around either pair.
All three marks share one unordered field, so those parenthesizations denote the same conjunction here. Account for each ring once; there is no reassociation operation to perform.
A written reader might group the first two alternatives or the last two. This closure gives all three branch veils the same parent field.
With no inner grouping boundary, both parenthesizations denote the same disjunction. Account for each ring-owned branch once; no reassociation operation is missing.

Begin with P, use the P‚ÜíQ link to release Q, then use that Q with the Q‚ÜíR link to release R; the middle owner is the handoff between the two implications, so compare owners rather than choosing a link by position.
This tablet extends the familiar composition chain by one link: P leads to Q, Q leads to R, and R leads to S. The repeated owner at each handoff tells you how the three implication chambers connect.
The final denied S cannot be answered from P directly. Let each implication make the next owned mark available, while remaining open to any valid order of independent cleanup.
Use the complete P‚àßS premise to obtain the complete Q‚àßT handoff. That entire pair is needed to reach R.

P and S enter the first implication together, but only P changes through Q into R. S is the side premise: it is needed to match the compound entrance and then remains unchanged while composition continues.
Do not confuse an unchanged premise with a useless one. Track where S is required, preserve it through the chain, and use any valid cleanup order once R is supported.
Two implication tracks run in parallel: P supports Q, and R supports S. The denied conclusion asks for Q and S together, so neither track can stand in for the other.
Lift each premise through its matching implication, then read the two produced marks as one conjunction. Any valid order between the independent tracks is welcome.

Lift independent compound-output implications together into one four-owner conjunction. Follow ownership and the visible branch or handoff structure; any legal route that completes the seal is welcome.
P leads to R, and Q also leads to R. The forked field says that one of P or Q holds, so the two implications are the two cases of one argument.
The terminal denied R can be compared with both case implications. Account for both alternatives before treating the fork as exhausted; symmetric branch orders are equally valid.
Resolve separate P, Q, and R implications to shared compound S‚àßT. Follow ownership and the visible branch or handoff structure; any legal route that completes the seal is welcome.
The implication changes P into Q. When the input is P or R, only the P branch follows that implication; R remains a valid alternative without being transformed.
The output is therefore Q or R. Keep the branch owners distinct, and use any valid route that accounts for both the mapped and preserved alternatives.
Map a compound P‚àßT branch to Q‚àßU while preserving compound R‚àßS. Follow ownership and the visible branch or handoff structure; any legal route that completes the seal is welcome.
P‚à®Q‚à®R is paired with P‚ÜíS, Q‚ÜíT, and R‚ÜíU, requiring three corresponding output alternatives S‚à®T‚à®U.
The standalone A is required, and that same owned A already satisfies the alternative A ‚à® B; collapse the repeated A and leave B as an irrelevant alternative to read A ‚àß (A ‚à® B) as A.
Treat P‚à®Q as one repeated whole. In (P‚à®Q)‚àß((P‚à®Q)‚à®(R‚àßS)), the second conjunct adds no requirement beyond that whole.
One alternative is P; the other is P together with Q. Both alternatives already contain the same P, so the added Q cannot prevent the disjunction from supporting P.
Treat Q as branch-specific material, then compare the common P across both cases. Any valid order that resolves the two alternatives is welcome.
Absorb a compound repeated P‚àßQ from (P‚àßQ)‚à®((P‚àßQ)‚àßR). Follow ownership and the visible branch or handoff structure; any legal route that completes the seal is welcome.
Read the right clause as the contrapositive of the left: reverse P‚ÜíQ to ¬¨Q‚Üí¬¨P, keeping the same P and Q owners while both endpoints cross polarity.
Contraposition reverses this implication, but the conjunctive endpoint remains one complete proposition while its polarity changes.
This is excluded middle around a two-mark conjunction. Treat the conjunction as one complete proposition and match it against its whole negation.
This larger excluded-middle seal surrounds a three-mark conjunction. The same whole-proposition pattern applies while all three marks remain inside the recognized group.
The denied target is the disjunction of the two separate negations. Give each atom its own double veil; only the completed pair matches that whole target.
This expansion has three peer components. Transform each one separately; the target exists only when all three negated alternatives are present.
Opening the negated disjunction yields one compound refusal and one atomic refusal. Keep the compound side intact: it and the atomic side feed different continuations before the results meet.
The two separately negated components are the source, not the finish. Open both double-veiled branches so they form the single negated conjunction required by the target.

The largest connected fragment is a coherent transitive chain, but it is irrelevant to the direct P/not-P complement beside it. The crowded fragment can be removed whole before resolving the simple core.

Peirce feedback ((P implies (Q and R)) implies P) implies P with an explicit compound feedback endpoint. Track complete owner, polarity, cut, and artifact topology; use the declared relationship as connected support for the remaining proof.

Peirce feedback with a conjunctive returned proposition P-and-R. Track complete owner, polarity, cut, and artifact topology; use the declared relationship as connected support for the remaining proof.
P, the required whole context S‚àßT, and the near-match S‚àßU sit beside a P‚àß(S‚àßT) to Q‚àß(S‚àßT) to R‚àß(S‚àßT) chain.
The enclosed P-and-Q source has one spare R mark. Remove R, then use the complete P-and-Q whole in the useful alternative branch; the exposed T continues onward to U.
Connected branch map into a side-bearing two-case chain. Follow ownership and the visible branch or handoff structure; any legal route that completes the seal is welcome.
P and Q prepare locally to R and S, converge separately to T, and only then share the single T‚ÜíU continuation.
The P branch follows P‚ÜíR‚ÜíT while the Q branch follows Q‚ÜíS‚ÜíT before the cases converge.
Releasing the outer double negation turns ¬¨(P‚à®Q) into the full product ¬¨P‚àß¬¨Q; the ¬¨P conjunct is then projected as the exact antecedent consumed by ¬¨P‚ÜíR.
The De Morgan alternatives enter separate case handlers and converge on R. That common result is still intermediate: carry R through to S before confronting the denied S.
The two P marks are not initially in a usable copy direction. Wrap only the deeper P: its new descendant cut matches the cut-delimited source, while Q or P+Q does not.
The existing positive fields are inside or beside the complete P-Q target, not above it. Wrap the whole target to create a positive ancestor annulus, then insert an exact P-Q double cut there.
The sole P source looks erasable, but both the P-to-Q and P-to-R branches depend on it. Preserve it until it has served both downstream occurrences.
Factor the complete P‚àßQ pair from both alternatives. The result has the form X‚àß(X‚à®(R‚à®S)); absorb the repeated X to finish.
Bring all three cases to two clauses sharing S. Factor S to expose S‚à®(S‚àßU), then absorb the repeated S.
The whole context K=U‚àßV is supplied outside P‚à®Q, the branches map to R and S, and K must then be recollected with each mapped result by the downstream consumers.

P now yields Q together with an extra side result S. Project the Q handoff from that richer intermediate, use Q with Q‚ÜíR, and keep S distinct while the chain reaches R; any legal cleanup order is welcome.
Match each complete occurrence to its exact introduction-path record: single-mark-return and nested-owner-introduction. Similar cut silhouettes are not interchangeable.
Dissolve the exact current single-mark artifact inside an atomic identity context
The Myratic hollow is deliberate. It asks for an entire seal-pattern. Open the loupe and place the blank sheet within it.
Precisely. To a Myratic seal, even an unwritten sheet is a complete pattern.
